threats.title: AgentVeToken stake() Allows Arbitrary Delegation Hijack of Any Receiver's Voting Power
threats.hypothesis_description: The stake() function accepts arbitrary `receiver` and `delegatee` parameters without verifying msg.sender == receiver. An attacker can call stake(1, victim, attackerValidator) to mint 1 wei of veToken to the victim while forcibly re-delegating the victim's ENTIRE veToken balance to the attacker's chosen validator, stealing all governance voting power.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _delegate(receiver, delegatee) call in stake() overwrites the receiver's entire delegation, not just the newly staked portion. Since any caller with 1 wei of the LP token can target any existing staker, this enables complete governance takeover of any agent DAO.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) called with user-supplied receiver without checking msg.sender == receiver; overrides entire balance delegation

preconditions: canStake == true (public agent mode)
Attacker holds >= 1 wei of the LP staking asset token
Victim has existing veToken balance with delegation to a different validator

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: stake() takes receiver and delegatee as separate parameters with no ownership check on receiver
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent - any block after victim has staked

operational_questions: How many agents have canStake=true with significant veToken staking?
What is the minimum LP token amount needed (obtainable via tiny liquidity provision)?
Can the victim re-delegate after attack, or can attacker repeat?
validation_requirements: Deploy test veToken, stake as Alice delegating to ValidatorA, then call stake(1, Alice, AttackerValidator) from attacker
Verify Alice's entire voting power moves to AttackerValidator
Confirm this works for any receiver address

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify target agent with valuable DAO governance and active stakers
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Obtain 1 wei of the LP staking asset (provide minimal liquidity to Uniswap pair)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Call stake(1, victimAddress, attackerValidator) for each major staker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: With majority voting power, pass malicious DAO proposals (setDAO, setCoreTypes, etc.)
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Single transaction per victim, no coordination needed

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete DAO governance takeover for any agent
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All stakers lose their delegation choice; attacker controls all governance votes
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Victims must re-stake or re-delegate, but attacker can repeat the attack
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All DAO-governed functions: setCoreTypes, setTokenURI, setDAO

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: 1 wei of LP token per victim targeted
execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: None - single attacker, single transaction per victim
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time after victims have staked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: stake() calls where msg.sender != receiver parameter
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden delegation changes for multiple addresses to same validator
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Rapid sequential stake calls with small amounts targeting different receivers

threats.title: AgentFactoryV4 Complete Asset Drain via Custom Token Aliasing with assetToken
threats.hypothesis_description: initFromToken() does not validate that tokenAddr != assetToken. An attacker can register assetToken as a custom token application. On withdraw, the factory first transfers the applicationThreshold of assetToken, then transfers the ENTIRE remaining assetToken balance (intended for other proposers) to the attacker as the 'custom token' refund.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function uses IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) to determine the custom token refund amount. When customToken == assetToken, this includes all other proposers' deposits, enabling a complete factory drain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: withdraw() transfers IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) without tracking deposited amount; no check that tokenAddr != assetToken in initFromToken

preconditions: _tokenApplication[assetToken] == 0 (assetToken not previously registered as custom token)
Factory holds assetToken deposits from other proposers (withdrawable amounts > 0)
Attacker has applicationThreshold + initialLP of assetToken

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: initFromToken has no require(tokenAddr != assetToken) check; isCompatibleToken only checks ERC20 interface
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any block; attacker waits 1 block for proposalEndBlock maturity

operational_questions: What is the current assetToken balance held by AgentFactoryV4?
How many active (pending) applications exist with withdrawable amounts?
Is _tokenApplication[assetToken] currently 0?
validation_requirements: Create application via initFromToken with tokenAddr = assetToken address
Wait 1 block, then call withdraw()
Verify attacker receives entire factory assetToken balance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Check factory assetToken balance to estimate profit
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call initFromToken(assetToken, cores, ..., initialLP=1) depositing applicationThreshold + 1 wei
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Wait 1 block (2 seconds on Base) for proposalEndBlock maturity
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Call withdraw(id) - receive applicationThreshold + entire remaining factory balance

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All pending applications become unable to execute (no liquidity) or withdraw (no funds)
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Funds are irrecoverably lost; all affected proposers lose their applicationThreshold deposits
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All pending proposeAgent and initFromToken applications

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: applicationThreshold + 1 wei of assetToken (returned as part of exploit)
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time factory holds other proposers' deposits

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: initFromToken call where tokenAddr matches assetToken address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden complete drain of factory assetToken balance
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: initFromToken followed immediately by withdraw in next block

threats.title: Permissionless addValidator Enables Validator Score Inflation and Governance Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentNftV2.addValidator() has no access control - anyone can call it to register any address as a validator for any virtualId. The _initValidatorScore function grants a base score equal to totalProposals at registration time, giving late-registering validators an inflated score equal to all past DAO proposals without any actual participation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Combined with the stake delegation hijack (OPS-H001), this creates a two-step governance capture: register as validator via addValidator, then use stake() to redirect voting power to the newly registered validator with an already-inflated score.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addValidator is public with no access control modifier; _initValidatorScore grants base score = totalProposals(virtualId)

preconditions: Target virtualId has been minted (agent exists)
Target agent has non-zero totalProposals for score inflation

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: addValidator has no onlyVirtualDAO, no onlyRole modifier, no caller validation at all

operational_questions: Which agents have high totalProposals values (maximum score inflation)?
Does totalUptimeScore rely on validator scores for reward distribution?
Can mass validator registration cause gas-based DoS on totalUptimeScore?
validation_requirements: Call addValidator(targetVirtualId, attackerAddress) from any EOA
Check validatorScore(targetVirtualId, attackerAddress) equals totalProposals
Verify validator array grows unboundedly

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify agents with many past proposals (high score inflation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call addValidator(virtualId, attackerAddr) to register and get instant high base score
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Optionally register thousands of addresses to grief totalUptimeScore (gas DoS)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Combine with OPS-H001 to capture governance via score-inflated validator

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Validator score manipulation affects governance weight calculations
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: totalUptimeScore becomes unreliable; DoS possible with many validators
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No mechanism to remove validators; validator array only grows
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Validator scoring, governance weight, reward distribution proportions

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Mass addValidator calls from unknown addresses
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Validator count growing abnormally for specific agents
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Addresses registering as validators across multiple agents simultaneously

threats.title: AgentToken Tax Rate Missing Upper Bound and Monotonicity Enforcement Enables Economic Exploitation
threats.hypothesis_description: setProjectTaxRates() is documented as 'subject to only ever decreasing' but enforces NO monotonicity constraint and NO upper bound validation. Tax rates can be increased to any uint16 value (up to 65535 BP = 655%). This is a code defect: the documented invariant is not implemented. Furthermore, rates >= BP_DENOM (10000) trigger an unchecked arithmetic underflow in _taxProcessing, creating phantom token balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The NatSpec comment explicitly states rates should only decrease. The code has zero enforcement. This gap between documented and implemented behavior constitutes a code vulnerability. The unchecked block in _taxProcessing means values >10000 BP cause catastrophic underflow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setProjectTaxRates has no require(new <= old) check despite comment 'subject to only ever decreasing'; _taxProcessing at lines 424-455 uses unchecked{amountLessTax_ -= tax} which underflows when tax > sentAmount

preconditions: Owner or factory calls setProjectTaxRates with values exceeding current rates or exceeding BP_DENOM

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Comment says 'only ever decreasing' but code performs direct assignment without comparison
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: onlyOwnerOrFactory required; code should self-enforce bounds regardless
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediate effect on all subsequent trades

operational_questions: Can factory contract be configured to automatically set tax rates?
What happens to existing trades in-flight when rates change?
Does the unchecked underflow at >10000 BP actually inflate recipient balance?
validation_requirements: Set tax rate to 10001 BP, execute a sell to LP, verify amountLessTax_ underflows
Verify recipient balance inflates to near uint256.max
Test monotonicity: set rate low, then try to increase

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner/factory sets tax rate to value >10000 BP (or even moderately high like 5000)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: For rates >10000: any sell to LP triggers underflow, minting massive tokens to recipient
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: For rates <10000 but increased: users trapped with higher tax than expected
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token economy destroyed or users gradually drained

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Complete token supply manipulation via underflow
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Token economy destroyed; all holders affected
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Irreversible - inflated balances cannot be recalled
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All token transfers involving liquidity pools

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Immediate impact on next LP trade

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: setProjectTaxRates calls with values >10000 or increasing from previous
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden massive balance increases on sell transactions
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Tax rate changes followed by large sells

threats.title: Bot Protection Duration Set But Never Enforced - Dead Code Renders Feature Non-Functional
threats.hypothesis_description: botProtectionDurationInSeconds is stored during AgentToken.initialize() but is never referenced in any transfer logic, validation, or any other function. The documented bot protection feature is completely non-functional dead code, allowing bots to trade immediately after initial liquidity is added.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable is set from supply parameters but never read. The _pretaxValidationAndLimits function only checks fundedDate for pre-funding protection but has no post-funding temporal restriction. This is a missing functionality defect.

proof_of_signal.pattern: botProtectionDurationInSeconds = supplyParams.botProtectionDurationInSeconds; variable is set but grep shows zero reads in transfer logic

preconditions: Any AgentToken instance is deployed with non-zero botProtectionDurationInSeconds

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: State variable stored in initialize but never referenced in _transfer, _pretaxValidationAndLimits, or any guard
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: None - this is a missing implementation issue
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Immediately after fundedDate is set (initial liquidity added)

operational_questions: Was bot protection intended as a core security feature for token launches?
What botProtectionDurationInSeconds values are configured in deployment scripts?
Are there deployed tokens relying on this non-functional protection?
validation_requirements: Deploy AgentToken with botProtectionDurationInSeconds = 3600
Add initial liquidity, immediately attempt to buy from LP
Verify buy succeeds with no temporal restriction

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for new AgentToken deployments and addInitialLiquidity calls
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Immediately execute buy transaction on the new LP pair
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Acquire tokens at initial price before organic traders
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Sell at higher price as organic demand arrives

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Every new agent token launch is vulnerable to sniping
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Cannot be fixed without contract upgrade

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: Capital to buy tokens on launch

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Consistent first-buyer addresses across multiple token launches
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Buy transactions in same block or immediately after addInitialLiquidity

threats.title: AgentFactoryV4 Sentinel Value Collision Enables Duplicate Custom Token Registration
threats.hypothesis_description: The _tokenApplication mapping uses 0 as the sentinel value for 'no application'. If _nextId is initialized to 0, the first initFromToken call creates application ID 0, making _tokenApplication[token] = 0 indistinguishable from 'not registered'. This allows duplicate registration of the same custom token, enabling double-withdrawal of the factory's token balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: AgentFactoryV4.initialize sets _nextId = nextId_. If nextId_ is 0, the sentinel check `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0` fails to detect that the token is already registered for application ID 0. Combined with the withdraw function sending entire balanceOf, this enables repeated drains.
threats.config_evidence: AgentFactoryV4.sol initialize: _nextId = nextId_; no validation that nextId_ > 0. Deployment script personaFactoryArguments.js doesn't show V4-specific init params.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0) uses 0 as sentinel; _nextId can be 0 making first application ID=0

preconditions: _nextId initialized to 0 in AgentFactoryV4.initialize()
First custom token application creates ID 0
That application is withdrawn (clearing _tokenApplication[token] = 0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Sentinel value 0 collides with valid application ID 0
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: After first application with ID 0 is withdrawn

operational_questions: What is the actual _nextId initialization value in production?
Has any application with ID 0 been created?
Could this also affect proposeAgent applications?
validation_requirements: Check deployed factory's _nextId initialization value
Test with nextId_=0: initFromToken, withdraw, initFromToken again with same token
Verify second registration succeeds despite token already being used

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Create first custom token application (gets ID 0), withdraw it
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Re-register same token via initFromToken (sentinel check passes since ID=0)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdraw again to drain factory's balance of that custom token

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Custom token tracking corrupted; duplicate applications possible
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires contract upgrade to fix sentinel logic

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After first custom token application is withdrawn

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Multiple initFromToken calls for the same token address
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Application ID 0 existing in the mapping

threats.title: AgentFactoryV2 Uses Unsafe IERC20.transfer Instead of SafeERC20 for Initial Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentFactoryV2.executeApplication uses IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount) instead of SafeERC20.safeTransfer. If assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 (like USDT) that returns false instead of reverting on failure, the transfer silently fails. The application proceeds with zero liquidity, creating a broken LP pool.
threats.investigation_rationale: V3 and V4 factories correctly use safeTransfer, but V2 does not. If V2 is still deployed and active, any application execution with a non-standard ERC20 assetToken would create an agent with zero initial liquidity, potentially allowing the first buyer to set an arbitrary price.
deployment_assumptions: assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false on failure
threats.config_evidence: AgentFactory.sol line: IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount); V3/V4 use safeTransfer. Deployment scripts show assetToken = process.env.BRIDGED_TOKEN.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(assetToken).transfer(token, initialAmount) - raw transfer without SafeERC20 wrapper; return value not checked

preconditions: AgentFactoryV2 is still actively used (not fully migrated to V3/V4)
assetToken is or could be changed to a non-standard ERC20
The non-standard token returns false on insufficient balance instead of reverting

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: V2 imports SafeERC20 and uses it for other transfers but not for this critical one

operational_questions: Is AgentFactoryV2 still deployed and accepting applications?
What is the actual assetToken and does it conform to standard ERC20?
Has setAssetToken ever been called to change to a non-standard token?
validation_requirements: Verify BRIDGED_TOKEN's transfer behavior on failure
Test executeApplication with a mock non-standard ERC20 that returns false
Confirm LP pool gets created with zero liquidity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Verify V2 factory is active and assetToken behavior
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: If assetToken returns false on fail: propose agent, ensure factory has insufficient balance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Execute application - transfer silently fails, LP created with zero assetToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Buy tokens at effectively zero price from the LP

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Broken agent launches with zero initial liquidity
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Agent must be redeployed; lost applicationThreshold

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Understanding of ERC20 return value semantics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: addInitialLiquidity calls with very low or zero pairToken amounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: LP pools with near-zero reserves on one side
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Application execution followed by immediate large buys at distorted prices

threats.title: AgentFactoryV4 Custom Token Withdraw Sends Entire Factory Balance Instead of Deposited Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: When withdrawing a custom token application, the factory transfers IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this)) instead of tracking and returning only the deposited initialLP amount. If extra tokens of the same type arrive at the factory (airdrops, direct transfers, or dust from other operations), the withdrawer receives unearned tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The withdraw function has no storage of the original initialLP deposit amount per application. It uses the entire factory balance as the withdrawal amount, violating the principle of returning only what was deposited.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(customToken).safeTransfer(application.proposer, IERC20(customToken).balanceOf(address(this))); uses balanceOf instead of tracked deposit amount

preconditions: Factory holds more of the custom token than the proposer originally deposited
Extra tokens arrive via airdrops, direct transfers, or accounting dust

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Application struct stores withdrawableAmount for assetToken but has no field for initialLP of custom token
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time between initFromToken and withdraw when extra tokens exist

operational_questions: Can tokens be sent directly to the factory to inflate the withdrawal amount?
Are there any other flows that deposit custom tokens to the factory?
Is this exploitable as a standalone attack by intentionally sending tokens first?
validation_requirements: Create custom token app, send extra custom tokens to factory, then withdraw
Verify withdrawer receives more than they deposited

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create initFromToken application with small initialLP
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Send additional custom tokens to factory address directly
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Withdraw application to receive initialLP + all extra tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Net gain equals the extra tokens sent minus gas costs
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Attacker must supply the extra tokens (so this is mainly a fairness/accounting issue unless combined with other vectors)

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Accounting discrepancy; factory loses tokens it doesn't track
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: No mechanism to recover misallocated tokens

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any time custom token application is active

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Withdraw returning more custom tokens than deposited in initFromToken
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Factory receiving custom token transfers from non-initFromToken sources
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Direct token transfers to factory followed by application withdrawal

threats.title: AgentVeToken Admin Can Bypass Founder Lock by Setting matureAt to Past Timestamp
threats.hypothesis_description: setMatureAt() allows any address with ADMIN_ROLE on agentNft to set the matureAt timestamp to any value, including 0 or past timestamps. This bypasses the founder's initialLock intended to protect stakers by ensuring the founder's LP cannot be withdrawn for the maturity duration (e.g., 10 years).
threats.investigation_rationale: The trust model states that founders' initial LP stake is locked until maturity to reassure other stakers. The admin can unilaterally circumvent this by calling setMatureAt(0), enabling immediate founder withdrawal and potential LP dump.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setMatureAt has no require(_matureAt >= matureAt) check; allows reducing lock period or setting to 0

preconditions: Attacker has ADMIN_ROLE on agentNft contract
Agent has founder with initialLock that hasn't matured yet

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setMatureAt performs direct assignment with no monotonicity check

operational_questions: Which addresses hold ADMIN_ROLE on agentNft?
What maturity durations are configured for deployed agents?
Has setMatureAt been called in production?
validation_requirements: Call setMatureAt(0) as admin, then founder calls withdraw(initialLock)
Verify withdrawal succeeds despite original maturity being years away

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Admin calls setMatureAt(0) for target veToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Founder calls withdraw(initialLock) to extract locked LP tokens
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Founder removes liquidity from Uniswap pair
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token price crashes; other stakers suffer losses

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: LP rug pull crashes token price
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All stakers of the affected agent

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Founder withdrawals before original maturity date
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: setMatureAt followed by large founder withdrawal

threats.title: AgentMigrator Owner Reset Enables Re-Migration Stranding Existing VeToken Stakers
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentMigrator.reset() allows the owner to clear the migratedAgents flag for any ID, enabling migrateAgent to be called again. Re-migration creates new token/DAO/veToken infrastructure and overwrites AgentNft mappings via migrateVirtual, stranding all existing veToken holders with tokens pointing to an orphaned DAO.
threats.investigation_rationale: The reset function makes migration non-final. After re-migration, old veToken stakers hold tokens whose agentNft mappings now point to new infrastructure. Their governance power, scoring, and ability to interact with the agent are severed.

proof_of_signal.pattern: reset(id) sets migratedAgents[id] = false; no check on existing staker state or veToken holders

preconditions: AgentMigrator owner calls reset(id) for a previously migrated agent
Founder calls migrateAgent(id, ...) again
Existing stakers hold veTokens from the first migration

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: reset allows reversing migration status with no checks on downstream state

operational_questions: Has reset() ever been called in production?
How many agents have been migrated with active stakers?
Can old veToken holders still withdraw their LP even after re-migration?
validation_requirements: Migrate agent, stake as third party, reset, re-migrate
Verify old stakers' veToken becomes disconnected from agent governance
Check if old stakers can still withdraw their LP tokens

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Founder calls migrateAgent(targetId, ...) creating new infrastructure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: migrateVirtual overwrites AgentNft mappings to new DAO/token/veToken
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Old stakers' veTokens now reference orphaned infrastructure; new founder gets fresh LP stake

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Agent governance taken over by new infrastructure
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: All existing stakers lose governance power and connection to agent
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Old veToken holders' LP may still be withdrawable but governance is lost
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All staking, governance, and scoring for the affected agent

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: After initial migration with active stakers

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: reset() calls on previously migrated IDs
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Multiple migrateAgent events for the same virtualId
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: reset followed by migrateAgent in quick succession

threats.title: AgentNftV2 setDAO Can Permanently Brick Agent Governance by Setting DAO to address(0)
threats.hypothesis_description: setDAO() allows the current DAO to change itself to any address including address(0), with no validation. If a malicious DAO proposal passes that calls setDAO(virtualId, address(0)), the agent's governance becomes permanently bricked since all onlyVirtualDAO-gated functions require msg.sender == address(0), which is impossible.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onlyVirtualDAO modifier checks _msgSender() == virtualInfos[virtualId].dao. If dao is set to address(0), no caller can ever satisfy this condition. setCoreTypes, setTokenURI, and setDAO itself all become permanently inaccessible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setDAO performs no validation on newDAO address; require(_msgSender() == virtualInfos[virtualId].dao) in modifier becomes unsatisfiable if dao == address(0)

preconditions: Attacker gains DAO governance majority (via OPS-H001 delegation hijack or legitimate voting power)
Malicious proposal to call AgentNftV2.setDAO(virtualId, address(0)) passes

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: setDAO has no require(newDAO != address(0)) and no require(newDAO is valid governor)
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time governance can be captured

operational_questions: Can ADMIN_ROLE override via migrateVirtual to recover from address(0) DAO?
What is the minimum voting power needed to pass a setDAO proposal?
Can this be chained with OPS-H001 for a low-cost governance bricking attack?
validation_requirements: Pass DAO proposal calling setDAO(virtualId, address(0))
Verify all onlyVirtualDAO functions become permanently inaccessible
Check if migrateVirtual can restore governance

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Gain DAO voting majority (via OPS-H001 or token accumulation)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Submit proposal to call setDAO(virtualId, address(0))
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vote proposal to pass and execute
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Agent governance is permanently bricked - no metadata updates possible
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Sufficient voting power; can be obtained via OPS-H001

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Cannot update agent metadata, core types, or token URI
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only recoverable if ADMIN_ROLE holder calls migrateVirtual
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: setCoreTypes, setTokenURI, setDAO - all permanently blocked

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: DAO proposals targeting setDAO with address(0) argument
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: DAO address changing to zero for any virtualId
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Delegation hijacking followed by governance proposals

threats.title: AgentMigrator Uses Unsafe IERC20.transferFrom for Asset Token Transfer During Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: AgentMigrator.migrateAgent uses raw IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount) without SafeERC20, despite importing SafeERC20 in other contracts. If _assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false on failure, the transfer silently fails and migration proceeds with zero initial liquidity, creating a broken agent.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is the same pattern as OPS-H007 in AgentFactoryV2 but in the migrator context. The deployer configures _assetToken via setInitParams, and if it's ever set to a non-standard ERC20, all migrations create broken LP pools.
deployment_assumptions: _assetToken is a non-standard ERC20 that returns false on failure
threats.config_evidence: AgentMigrator.sol line: IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount); deployment script v2/deployMigrator.ts sets assetToken = process.env.BRIDGED_TOKEN

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_assetToken).transferFrom(founder, token, initialAmount) - raw transferFrom without SafeERC20

preconditions: AgentMigrator is actively used for migrations
_assetToken is or could be changed to a non-standard ERC20

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Raw IERC20.transferFrom used instead of SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom

operational_questions: What is the actual _assetToken used in production?
Does it conform to standard ERC20 return value behavior?
Are migrations still occurring?
validation_requirements: Test migrateAgent with a mock non-standard ERC20 that returns false
Verify migration proceeds with zero initial liquidity

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Verify _assetToken returns false on failed transfer
